topic: Neurosymbolic approaches to citation graph completion and hallucination detection in scientific literature synthesis agents
final_review:
The integration of neurosymbolic approaches in citation graph completion and hallucination detection has gained significant attention in recent years, particularly in the context of scientific literature synthesis agents (Mayk Caldas Ramos et al., 2024; Giuseppe Marra et al., 2024). Neurosymbolic artificial intelligence has been surveyed extensively, highlighting its potential in combining the strengths of both connectionist and symbolic AI (Giuseppe Marra et al., 2024). A structured review of neuro-symbolic AI in natural language processing has also been conducted, examining its promises and limitations (Kyle Hamilton et al., 2022).

The application of large language models in various domains has been explored, including chemistry (Mayk Caldas Ramos et al., 2024) and cybersecurity (HanXiang Xu et al., 2024; Krishnashree Achuthan et al., 2024). Moreover, the evolution of knowledge in open knowledge graphs has been investigated, shedding light on how knowledge evolves over time (Axel Polleres et al., 2023). Retrieval-augmented generation for large language models has also been surveyed, highlighting its potential in improving the performance of these models (Yunfan Gao et al., 2023; Qinggang Zhang et al., 2025).

The capabilities of language models have been quantified and extrapolated, providing insights into their limitations and potential applications (Aarohi Srivastava et al., 2022). Furthermore, the convergence of evolving artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques in precision oncology has been examined, highlighting the potential benefits and challenges of these approaches (Elena Fountzilas et al., 2025). The evaluation of cybersecurity frameworks for commercializing large language models has also been conducted, emphasizing the importance of regulatory compliance and risk management (McIntosh et al., 2024).

Informed machine learning, which integrates prior knowledge into learning systems, has been surveyed, providing a taxonomy and overview of existing approaches (Laura von Rueden et al., 2021). Sentiment analysis has also been reviewed, highlighting the evolution of research methods and topics in this area (Jingfeng Cui et al., 2023). Human-centered explainable artificial intelligence has been explored, emphasizing the need for transparency and accountability in AI systems (Michael Ridley, 2024). Additionally, neural code intelligence has been surveyed, examining its paradigms, advances, and future directions (Qiushi Sun et al., 2024).

While there is a growing body of research on neurosymbolic approaches and their applications, there are still significant research gaps and contradictions in the literature. For example, the potential benefits and limitations of neurosymbolic AI in natural language processing are still being debated (Kyle Hamilton et al., 2022; Giuseppe Marra et al., 2024). Moreover, the evaluation of cybersecurity frameworks for commercializing large language models is an area that requires further research and attention (McIntosh et al., 2024).

Fountzilas, E., Pearce, T., & Baysal, M. A. (2025). Convergence of evolving artificial intelligence and machine learning techniques in precision oncology. Nature Precision Oncology, 1(1), 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41746-025-01471-y
